content.full_text:
ROBERT D. HANSEN
Attorney At Lav,,
FrPC
FEB 1 : 1992
661 Pamlar Avenue
San Jose, California 95 128
(408) 287-1500
February 7,1992
Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Acting GeneralCounsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428'J' Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814
Dear Mr. Hallabrin,
I serve as legal counsel to an unsuccessful candidate for mayor of the City of San Jose. During
the past weeks I have had several conversations with Ms. Linda Morrow of the Fair Political Practices
Commission ("the Commission") staff about the committee's efforts to retire various accrued expenses
from the campaign. She was gracious enough to fonruard me a copy of a June 28,1977 letter to Mr. Robert
C, Summers from the Commission regarding the reporting requirements of vendors that forgive debts
owed to them by political campaigns. The letter and a recent court decision raises several unresolved
matters about our committee's efforts and I am writing to you for the Commission's opinions that might
hopefully clarify the matters.
The candidate formed a controlled political committee for the 1990 June primary and November
general elections for the office of mayor of the City of San Jose. The committee was involved only in the
local municipal race and had no involvement in any state legislative, constitutional office, or initiative
campaigns. Many hundreds of thousands of dollars were raised during the course of the campaign and
the money was used to pay for all of the expenses of the committee except for approximately $80,000 in
accrued expenses from the primary election that is still owed to the candidate, a campaign consultant, and
to a local company for the rental of office space. The City of San Jose is a charter city under California law
and has enacted a municipal ordinance limiting contributions to a mayoral candidate to no more than
$5oo.oo in any fiscal year from any one individual. All of the appropriate campaign reports have been filed
in a timely manner wiin tne San Jose City Clerk's office and the committee has fully complied with the San
Jose Code and California law.
The committee still exists today but it is for the most part inactive. For a variety of reasons,
including the candidate's desire to seek a city council seat during the 1992 elections and the formation of a
second ind separate controlled committee for those campaigns, the mayoral committee may not be able to
retire the 1gg0 primary campaign debt. The mayoral committee, however, appears to have four options for
reducing the debt: fiist, raising additional funds and paying the debts over an e>ltended period of time;
second, the creditors contributing portions of the accrued expenses as non-monetary contributions to the
campaign while not exceeding local contribution limits; third, complete or partial default by the committee
upon thie obligations to pay tne debts; or fourth, negotiating a reduced value of the debts based on
externalfactors and commercial market conditions. lt is problematic if any one approach or a combination of
the options will be successful in retiring all or any portion of the debts.


Mr. Scott Hallabrin
February 7, 1992
Page 2
To aid the committee in its evaluation ol these options, we are requesting the Commission's
advice on the following questions:
1) 
The Commission's June 28, 1977 letter to Mr. Summers refers to "bona ride business
iudgement that all or part of the debt is uncollectable." Can you provide guidance as to what factors or
tests are considered by the Commission in determining what constitutes a "bona lide business
iudgement?"
2) 
The Commission's June 28, 1 977 letter provides guidance to the creditors in
determining the creditor's Jiling obligations upon forgiveness or reduction of a debt. ls the letter also
applicable to a debtor committee in determining its obligations and options?
On a related matter, I have been ,ollowing the progress ot Service Employees lntemational
Union. AFL-CIO, et al. v. Fai Political Practices Commission, Case No. CIVS 89-0433 LKK-JFM. The City
of San Jose's limits on campaign contributions makes it very difficult ,or the mayoral committee to reduce its
debts if any portion of the rorgiven or uncollectable debt exceeds the fiscal year limit on contributions. ln
light of today's decision by the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals in the case, can the Commission provide any
comments as to the constitutionality oI contribution limits lor campaigns governed by Caltfornia law in
general, as well as factors affecting the constitutionality of the City of San Jose's campaign contribution
limits?
lvery much appreciate your attention to my request. Should you have any questions about this
letter or desire additional facts in developing the Commission's response, please contact me at telephone
(408) 287-1 500. Written information can also be transmitted to me at facsimile line (408) 287-'1 536. Again,
thank you for your assistance.
Sincerely,ffi
Bobert D. Hansen
Attorney At Law
RDH:ls

sections.question: 1) The Commission's June 28, 1977 letter to Mr. Summers refers to "bona fide business judgement that all or part of the debt is uncollectable." Can you provide guidance as to what factors or tests are considered by the Commission in determining what constitutes a "bona fide business judgement?" 2) The Commission's June 28, 1977 letter provides guidance to the creditors in determining the creditor's filing obligations upon forgiveness or reduction of a debt. Is the letter also applicable to a debtor committee in determining its obligations and options? On a related matter, I have been following the progress of Service Employees International Union, AFL-CIO, et al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission, Case No. CIVS 89-0433 LKK-JFM. The City of San Jose's limits on campaign contributions makes it very difficult for the mayoral committee to reduce its debts if any portion of the forgiven or uncollectable debt exceeds the fiscal year limit on contributions. In light of today's decision by the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals in the case, can the Commission provide any comments as to the constitutionality of contribution limits for campaigns governed by California law in general, as well as factors affecting the constitutionality of the City of San Jose's campaign contribution limits?
sections.question_synthetic: The requester seeks FPPC guidance on how to evaluate debt forgiveness under the bona fide business judgement standard, whether prior FPPC guidance applies to debtor committees, and how a recent Ninth Circuit decision affects the constitutionality of San Jose's campaign contribution limits in the context of retiring campaign debts.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from outside counsel, not an FPPC response. No formal CONCLUSION or SHORT ANSWER section present. Document contains three related questions about campaign debt retirement, vendor debt forgiveness standards, and contribution limit constitutionality. No withdrawal, declination, or other closure indicated.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: 1) The Commission's June 28, 1977 letter to Mr. Summers refers to "bona fide business judgement that all or part of the debt is uncollectable." Can you provide guidance as to what factors or tests are considered by the Commission in determining what constitutes a "bona fide business judgement?" 2) The Commission's June 28, 1977 letter provides guidance to the creditors in determining the creditor's filing obligations upon forgiveness or reduction of a debt. Is the letter also applicable to a debtor committee in determining its obligations and options? On a related matter, I have been following the progress of Service Employees International Union, AFL-CIO, et al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission, Case No. CIVS 89-0433 LKK-JFM. The City of San Jose's limits on campaign contributions makes it very difficult for the mayoral committee to reduce its debts if any portion of the forgiven or uncollectable debt exceeds the fiscal year limit on contributions. In light of today's decision by the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals in the case, can the Commission provide any comments as to the constitutionality of contribution limits for campaigns governed by California law in general, as well as factors affecting the constitutionality of the City of San Jose's campaign contribution limits?

embedding.summary: An attorney representing an unsuccessful 1990 San Jose mayoral campaign requests FPPC advice on options for retiring approximately $80,000 in accrued campaign debts, specifically regarding bona fide business judgement standards for debt forgiveness, applicability of prior FPPC guidance to debtor committees, and constitutional implications of local contribution limits in light of a recent Ninth Circuit decision.
